enrichment.short_description: Clearbit was a B2B data enrichment and company/contact intelligence platform founded in 2014 in San Francisco. HubSpot acquired Clearbit in December 2023 and has since folded its enrichment technology into HubSpot as Breeze Intelligence, part of the broader Breeze AI suite. As of 2026 the standalone Clearbit product is retired for new customers, with enrichment, buyer intent, and form-shortening features sold only as a HubSpot add-on.
enrichment.pricing_model: Credit-based add-on bundled with a required paid HubSpot subscription (no longer sold as a standalone product)
enrichment.starting_price: Approximately 75 USD per month total (about 30 USD per month for HubSpot Starter plus about 45 USD per month for 100 Breeze Intelligence credits on annual billing)
enrichment.company_size: Now part of HubSpot (which has over 7000 employees); the original Clearbit team was under 200 people prior to the 2023 acquisition
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (original headquarters; now operated as part of HubSpot, headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clearbit is now Breeze Intelligence under HubSpot. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons of this B2B data enrichment and intent platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clearbit is a B2B data enrichment and company/contact intelligence platform originally founded in 2014 in San Francisco, California by Alex MacCaw, Harlow Ward, and Rob Holland. For nearly a decade it operated as an independent company, raising seed and Series A venture funding and becoming a widely used integration inside sales and marketing stacks built on Salesforce, HubSpot, Marketo, and Segment. Clearbit's core technology enriched partial records, such as an email address or a company domain, into full firmographic and technographic profiles: employee count, industry, estimated revenue, funding history, technology stack, job title, and seniority. In December 2023, HubSpot announced the acquisition of Clearbit, and throughout 2024 the standalone Clearbit product was progressively folded into HubSpot's Breeze AI suite under the new name Breeze Intelligence. As of 2026, Clearbit no longer exists as an independently purchasable SaaS product; new customers cannot sign up for clearbit.com directly, and the technology is sold exclusively as a credit-based add-on that requires an active paid HubSpot subscription (Starter, Professional, or Enterprise) as a prerequisite. Breeze Intelligence's core capabilities carried over from Clearbit include automatic enrichment of HubSpot CRM company and contact records, buyer intent signals that flag accounts showing increased research or engagement activity, form shortening that pre-fills known contact fields to reduce friction on HubSpot forms, and company-level visitor identification (often called Reveal) that deanonymizes anonymous website traffic down to the company level. Pricing operates on a monthly credit pool: entry-level packages of around 100 credits cost roughly 45 to 50 USD per month depending on annual versus monthly billing, mid-tier packages of 1000 credits run around 150 USD per month, and larger 10000-credit packages run around 700 USD per month; these costs are on top of the mandatory HubSpot subscription, which starts around 30 USD per month for Starter. Combined, minimum realistic total spend for a small team lands around 75 USD per month, while mid-market teams on HubSpot Professional commonly spend 1000 to 5000 USD or more per month once they add sufficient enrichment volume. Credits expire monthly with no rollover, which is a common source of billing confusion for teams evaluating total cost of ownership post-acquisition. The most significant practical implication for buyers researching Clearbit in 2026 is that it is functionally no longer available to teams outside the HubSpot ecosystem; organizations using Salesforce alone, Pipedrive, or other CRMs without a parallel HubSpot subscription cannot access the current version of the technology, and commonly evaluate alternatives such as ZoomInfo, Apollo.io, Cognism, or Clay instead. For HubSpot-native teams, however, Breeze Intelligence represents a natural extension of the platform they already use daily, with the enrichment, intent, and identification features appearing directly inside familiar HubSpot workflows, lead-scoring rules, and form builders rather than requiring a separate tool and a separate login. Anyone searching for Clearbit pricing, the Clearbit API, or Clearbit alternatives in 2026 should understand this rebrand and bundling change as the central fact governing how the product can now be bought and used.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Clearbit to Breeze Intelligence
paragraphs: Clearbit launched in 2014 as a standalone B2B data enrichment company, building a reputation for its developer-friendly APIs that appended firmographic, technographic, and contact data to email addresses, domains, and IP addresses. Over the following decade it became a staple integration for sales and marketing teams working inside Salesforce, HubSpot, Marketo, and Segment.

In December 2023, HubSpot announced it had acquired Clearbit, and by 2024 the product had been absorbed into HubSpot's Breeze AI suite under the name Breeze Intelligence. The standalone clearbit.com product and self-serve signup were retired for new customers, and as of 2026 enrichment, buyer intent, and form-shortening capabilities are sold exclusively as an add-on to a paid HubSpot subscription rather than as an independent SaaS product.

long_form_blocks.heading: How the enrichment technology works today
paragraphs: Breeze Intelligence still relies on the underlying Clearbit data engine: a combination of first-party crawling, public records, partner data, and machine learning models that assemble structured company and contact profiles from a fragmented web. Enrichment happens directly inside HubSpot CRM records, forms, and workflows, so marketing and sales teams see enriched fields (employee count, industry, revenue range, technology stack, job title, seniority) without leaving the HubSpot interface.

The product is priced on a credit system: each successful enrichment or intent lookup consumes one credit from a monthly pool that resets and does not roll over. Buyers must already hold a paid HubSpot Starter, Professional, or Enterprise seat before they can purchase Breeze Intelligence credits, which represents a meaningful shift from Clearbit's old plug-and-play pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who still uses it and alternatives
paragraphs: Existing HubSpot customers who valued Clearbit's enrichment accuracy are the primary remaining users, since the tool now only makes sense within the HubSpot ecosystem. Teams on other CRMs, or teams that want enrichment decoupled from a marketing platform subscription, generally evaluate alternatives such as Clay, ZoomInfo, Apollo.io, or Cognism instead.

features.title: Company and contact data enrichment
features.description: Appends firmographic fields (industry, employee count, revenue range, funding, technology stack) to HubSpot company and contact records.

features.title: Buyer intent signals
features.description: Surfaces accounts showing increased engagement or research activity so sales teams can prioritize outreach.

features.title: Form shortening
features.description: Auto-fills known contact fields on HubSpot forms so visitors see fewer fields, improving conversion rates.

features.title: Visitor identification (Reveal)
features.description: Deanonymizes company-level traffic hitting a website, matching anonymous visits to company records.

features.title: Native HubSpot workflows integration
features.description: Enrichment can trigger inside HubSpot automation workflows and lead-scoring rules.

features.title: Technographic data
features.description: Identifies the software and tools a target company already uses.

features.title: Multi-source data sourcing
features.description: Combines multiple underlying data sources to improve match and fill rates versus a single provider.

features.title: Credit-based usage dashboard
features.description: Lets admins monitor and forecast monthly enrichment credit consumption inside HubSpot.

pros: Deep native integration with HubSpot CRM, forms, and workflows removes the need for a separate enrichment tool if you are already on HubSpot
Data quality and match rates are generally considered strong, inheriting the original Clearbit data engine
Form shortening measurably improves conversion rates on gated content and demo request forms
Buyer intent data helps sales teams prioritize accounts that are actively researching solutions
Enrichment happens automatically inside existing HubSpot workflows, reducing manual data entry
cons: No longer available as a standalone product; a paid HubSpot subscription is now mandatory just to purchase credits
Total cost of ownership has increased significantly since the HubSpot acquisition, with mid-market teams often paying well over 1000 USD per month
Monthly credits expire with no rollover, which can waste unused capacity
Teams on non-HubSpot CRMs have much more limited access to the technology than before the acquisition
Pricing has become more layered and harder to predict, combining HubSpot tier costs with separate credit-pack costs

faqs.question: Is Clearbit still a separate product in 2026?
faqs.answer: No. Clearbit has been rebranded and folded into HubSpot as Breeze Intelligence, and it is no longer sold or signed up for as a standalone product.

faqs.question: Do I need HubSpot to use Clearbit or Breeze Intelligence?
faqs.answer: Yes. As of 2026, Breeze Intelligence requires an active paid HubSpot subscription before enrichment credits can be purchased.

faqs.question: What happened to the Clearbit API for developers?
faqs.answer: HubSpot has wound down the general-purpose standalone Clearbit APIs for new customers in favor of the in-product Breeze Intelligence enrichment features inside HubSpot.

faqs.question: How much does Breeze Intelligence cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is credit-based, with entry packages around 45 to 50 USD per month for 100 credits on top of a required HubSpot subscription that starts around 30 USD per month, though actual total spend varies by plan and volume.

faqs.question: What can Breeze Intelligence do that plain HubSpot forms cannot?
faqs.answer: It enriches contact and company records automatically, shortens forms by pre-filling known fields, identifies anonymous website visitors at the company level, and surfaces buyer intent signals.

faqs.question: What are good alternatives if I am not on HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Teams outside the HubSpot ecosystem commonly evaluate ZoomInfo, Apollo.io, Cognism, or Clay for comparable enrichment and intent capabilities.

faqs.question: Did the underlying data quality change after the acquisition?
faqs.answer: The core enrichment data engine originates from the original Clearbit technology, though it is now positioned and packaged under HubSpot's Breeze AI branding.

faqs.question: Can existing Clearbit API customers keep their old integration?
faqs.answer: HubSpot has been migrating legacy Clearbit customers toward Breeze Intelligence and HubSpot subscriptions rather than maintaining the old standalone product indefinitely, so long-term continuity of the old API is not guaranteed.

use_cases.title: B2B lead enrichment for sales teams
use_cases.description: Sales development reps enrich inbound leads with firmographic and contact data directly inside HubSpot so they can prioritize and personalize outreach without manual research.
use_cases.audience: B2B sales and revenue operations teams on HubSpot

use_cases.title: Shortening marketing forms
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use form shortening to auto-fill known fields for returning visitors, reducing friction and improving demo-request and content-download conversion rates.
use_cases.audience: Demand generation and marketing operations teams

use_cases.title: Identifying anonymous website traffic
use_cases.description: Marketing and sales teams use visitor identification to see which companies are browsing their site even before a lead form is filled out, enabling account-based follow-up.

pricing_plans.price: Approximately 45 USD per month (annual) or 50 USD per month (monthly)

enrichment.short_description: Clementine is a free, open-source, cross-platform music player for Windows, macOS, and Linux, forked from Amarok 1.4 in 2010 amid user backlash to Amarok 2's redesign. Built with Qt and GStreamer, it offers local music library management, smart playlists, podcast support, and streaming integrations. Development slowed after 2016 but resumed on GitHub, and a more actively maintained fork called Strawberry Music Player was created in 2018.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free, open source (GNU General Public License), no paid tiers
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer-run open source project, not a company
enrichment.seo_title: Clementine Music Player: Free Open-Source App 2026
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clementine is a free, open-source cross-platform music player forked from Amarok. See features, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clementine is a free, open-source, cross-platform music player for Windows, macOS, and Unix-like operating systems including most Linux distributions. It began in February 2010 as a fork of Amarok 1.4, created by developers David Sansome, John Maguire, and Arnaud Bienner after a significant portion of the Amarok user community pushed back against the ground-up rewrite that became Amarok 2, which removed or changed workflows longtime users relied on. By forking the last version of the classic Amarok codebase and porting it to the Qt 4 application framework alongside the GStreamer (and later Xine) multimedia backend, Clementine preserved the familiar three-pane library browser, tag-based organization, and playlist-first interface while continuing independent development. The project built a loyal following through the early-to-mid 2010s and became one of the more widely packaged third-party audio players across Linux distribution repositories, praised for combining a lightweight footprint with a feature set that rivaled larger commercial players. Clementine's core functionality centers on local music library management: it indexes large collections of audio files, supports tagging and metadata editing, and lets users build both manually curated and rule-based smart or dynamic playlists. Beyond local playback, it integrates with internet radio and streaming services such as SoundCloud and Last.fm via API keys, includes a built-in podcast search and download feature, offers an audio transcoder for converting between formats, supports syncing music to iPods and similar portable devices in a manner reminiscent of iTunes, and displays a real-time music visualization engine alongside lyrics and now-playing statistics. Development slowed considerably after the last stable release in 2016, with the project effectively entering a multi-year hiatus punctuated only by release candidate builds published to GitHub by remaining volunteer contributors. This slowdown led directly to the creation of Strawberry Music Player in 2018, a more actively maintained fork built for compatibility with the newer Qt 6 framework, gapless and high-resolution audio playback, and a trimmed set of legacy dependencies; many users seeking frequent updates migrated to Strawberry over the following years. Despite this, Clementine was not abandoned: on 15 October 2024 the project published version 1.4.1, its first non-release-candidate stable build since 2016, signaling renewed if still modest ongoing maintenance. As of 2026, Clementine remains entirely free to download and use under the GNU General Public License, with no subscription tiers, advertisements, or paid feature gates of any kind. It continues to appeal most to users who value its original, unmodified interface, prefer offline-first local library management over cloud-only streaming apps, and are comfortable with a slower, community-driven release cadence rather than the corporate-backed update schedules of commercial streaming applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Origins: a fork born from user backlash
paragraphs: Clementine began in February 2010 when developers David Sansome, John Maguire, and Arnaud Bienner forked Amarok 1.4, the beloved KDE 3-era music player, after many users felt the rewritten Amarok 2 had removed familiar features and workflows. Built on Qt 4 and the GStreamer (later also Xine) multimedia backend, Clementine kept the classic three-pane library browser and playlist-centric interface that longtime Amarok users wanted.

The project reached a stable 1.0 release and grew a loyal following on Linux, Windows, and macOS through the mid-2010s, becoming one of the most widely packaged third-party music players in Linux distribution repositories.

long_form_blocks.heading: Development status and the Strawberry fork
paragraphs: Clementine's last stable release before a long hiatus was in 2016, and active development largely paused. Community interest kept the codebase alive on GitHub through release candidates, and on 15 October 2024 the project published version 1.4.1, its first non-release-candidate stable build since 2016, showing the project remains maintained by volunteers rather than abandoned.

In 2018, a contributor created Strawberry Music Player, a more actively maintained fork of Clementine focused on Qt 6 compatibility, gapless and high-resolution audio playback, and trimming legacy dependencies. Many users who want ongoing updates now choose Strawberry, while Clementine remains popular with users who prefer its original, unmodified interface and are comfortable with a slower release cadence.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Clementine actually does
paragraphs: At its core, Clementine indexes a local music library and lets users build smart and dynamic playlists, tag and organize files, and browse by artist, album, or genre. It also connects to a range of internet radio and streaming services, supports podcast search and download, can transcode audio between formats, and can sync music to iPods and other portable devices. A visualization engine and lyrics/song-info panel round out the listening experience.

features.title: Local music library management
features.description: Indexes and organizes large local audio collections with tagging support.

features.title: Smart and dynamic playlists
features.description: Builds playlists based on rules, ratings, or listening history rather than manual curation only.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Unix-like/Linux systems.

features.title: Audio format transcoding
features.description: Converts between common audio formats using a built-in transcoder.

features.title: Podcast search and download
features.description: Finds and downloads podcast episodes directly inside the player.

features.title: Streaming service integration
features.description: Connects to services such as SoundCloud and internet radio via API keys.

features.title: iPod and device syncing
features.description: Mimics iTunes-style syncing to transfer music to iPods and similar devices.

features.title: Music visualization and lyrics display
features.description: Shows real-time visualizations alongside lyrics and now-playing statistics.

pros: Completely free and open source with no ads, subscriptions, or paid tiers
Familiar three-pane interface that longtime Amarok 1.4 users find easy to adopt
Strong local library management, tagging, and smart playlist features
Cross-platform availability across Windows, macOS, and Linux
Lightweight compared to many modern streaming-first music apps
cons: Development has historically been slow and inconsistent, with multi-year gaps between stable releases
Some streaming integrations have degraded or broken over time as third-party APIs changed
The Qt 4-derived interface can feel dated compared to modern music player designs
The more actively maintained Strawberry fork has drawn away part of the contributor community
Limited built-in support for newer high-resolution or lossless streaming standards compared to newer competitors

faqs.question: Is Clementine still being developed in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes, though slowly; a stable 1.4.1 release arrived in October 2024 after an eight-year gap, and the project continues on GitHub with volunteer maintainers.

faqs.question: Is Clementine free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clementine is free, open-source software released under the GNU General Public License with no paid version.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Clementine and Strawberry Music Player?
faqs.answer: Strawberry is a 2018 fork of Clementine built for Qt 6 with more active development, gapless playback improvements, and fewer legacy dependencies, while Clementine retains the original codebase and interface.

faqs.question: Does Clementine support streaming services like Spotify?
faqs.answer: Clementine has historically supported streaming integrations including Spotify, SoundCloud, and Last.fm through API keys, though availability of specific integrations can change as third-party APIs evolve.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Clementine run on?
faqs.answer: Clementine runs on Windows, macOS, and Unix-like systems including most Linux distributions.

faqs.question: Can Clementine play podcasts?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clementine includes built-in podcast search and download functionality.

faqs.question: Who created Clementine?
faqs.answer: Clementine was created by David Sansome along with contributors John Maguire and Arnaud Bienner, forking it from Amarok 1.4 in 2010.

faqs.question: Does Clementine sync with iPods?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clementine can interact with iPod devices to transfer and manage music, similar to iTunes.

use_cases.title: Managing a large local music library
use_cases.description: Users with extensive local MP3 or FLAC collections use Clementine to tag, organize, and browse thousands of tracks with smart playlists.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform desktop listening
use_cases.description: Users who switch between Windows, macOS, and Linux machines use Clementine for a consistent player experience across all of them.

use_cases.title: Lightweight alternative to cloud-first players
use_cases.description: Users who prefer offline-first, ad-free playback without a subscription choose Clementine over streaming-service-only apps.

enrichment.short_description: Clever is a K-12 single sign-on (SSO) and data integration platform used by the large majority of U.S. school districts to give students and teachers one-click access to hundreds of educational apps. Founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Francisco, Clever was acquired by Norway-based edtech company Kahoot! in September 2021 for an enterprise value of up to 500 million USD. As of 2026 Clever continues operating as a Kahoot! company, syncing student information system data and providing badge or password-based login for classrooms.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free for schools and districts; Clever generates revenue primarily through relationships with the education-app developers who integrate with its platform
enrichment.starting_price: Free for K-12 schools and districts
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 300 to 350 employees (part of the Kahoot! group)
enrichment.seo_title: Clever: K-12 SSO and Data Platform Guide 2026
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clever is the K-12 single sign-on and data integration platform used by most US school districts, now owned by Kahoot!. See features and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clever is a single sign-on (SSO) and data integration platform purpose-built for United States K-12 education, founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Its core function is connecting a school or district's student information system (SIS), such as PowerSchool or Infinite Campus, to the dozens or hundreds of separate educational software applications a district may use, then providing every student and teacher with a single set of login credentials that grants access to all of them. Rather than students needing to remember separate usernames and passwords for a reading app, a math platform, a learning management system, and more, Clever synchronizes roster, enrollment, and class-assignment data automatically and lets users click through to any connected application from a single Clever Portal dashboard. For younger students who cannot yet type or read reliably, Clever also supports physical badge-based or QR-code-based login, a widely cited differentiator versus generic enterprise SSO tools that assume adult, credential-literate users. Clever grew quickly to become one of the most widely deployed K-12 SSO and rostering platforms in the United States, with reported usage in the substantial majority of American school districts and roughly 20 million monthly users around the time of its acquisition. In May 2021, Norway-headquartered game-based learning company Kahoot! announced its intent to acquire Clever, and the transaction closed in September 2021 at an enterprise value of up to 500 million USD, paid through a combination of approximately 82 percent cash and 18 percent Kahoot! shares, with an additional performance-based earnout tied to 2021 to 2022 results. The acquisition combined Kahoot!'s classroom engagement and quiz-based learning tools with Clever's deep, district-level data and authentication infrastructure, positioning the combined group as a broader player across K-12 learning technology. As of 2026, Clever continues to operate under its own brand as part of the Kahoot! group, employing roughly 300 to 350 people globally. It remains free for schools and districts to adopt; Clever's revenue model instead centers on relationships with the education-app developers who integrate with its Application Gallery and Clever Instant Login authentication standard, effectively monetizing the supply side of its marketplace rather than charging the schools it serves. Data privacy and compliance, particularly with regulations like FERPA governing student educational records, are central to Clever's value proposition, since the platform sits in a position of trust handling sensitive student directory information across an entire district. It is worth explicitly noting that Clever, as a generic English word, is also used as a name by other unrelated products and companies in different industries; this profile refers specifically to Clever Inc., the K-12 education single sign-on and rostering company headquartered in San Francisco and now owned by Kahoot!, not to any other unrelated product sharing the same common name.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Clever does for K-12 schools
paragraphs: Clever is a single sign-on (SSO) and data integration platform built specifically for K-12 education. It connects to a school or district's student information system (SIS), such as PowerSchool or Infinite Campus, and automatically syncs roster and enrollment data to give every student and teacher one set of credentials that unlocks hundreds of educational applications.

Rather than requiring students to remember dozens of separate usernames and passwords for reading apps, math platforms, and learning management systems, Clever lets them log in once with a badge, QR code, or simple password and then click through to any connected app through the Clever Portal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Acquisition by Kahoot!
paragraphs: Founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Francisco, Clever grew to become one of the most widely deployed SSO and rostering platforms in United States K-12 education. In May 2021, Norway-based edtech company Kahoot! announced it would acquire Clever, and the deal closed in September 2021 at an enterprise value of up to 500 million USD, combining Kahoot!'s game-based learning tools with Clever's district-level data and login infrastructure.

As of 2026, Clever continues to operate under the Clever brand as part of the Kahoot! group, and it remains free for schools and districts to adopt, with Clever generating revenue primarily through its relationships with the education-app developers who integrate into its application gallery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Disambiguation: which Clever is this
paragraphs: The name Clever is also used by unrelated products and services in other industries, including consumer and enterprise software with similar generic branding. This entry refers specifically to Clever Inc., the K-12 education single sign-on and data platform headquartered in San Francisco and now owned by Kahoot!, not to any other unrelated product that happens to share the name.

features.title: Single sign-on for K-12 apps
features.description: Gives students and teachers one login that unlocks hundreds of connected educational applications.

features.title: Student information system integration
features.description: Automatically syncs roster, enrollment, and class data from systems like PowerSchool and Infinite Campus.

features.title: Badge and QR code login for young students
features.description: Lets pre-readers log in using a physical badge or scannable code instead of typing credentials.

features.title: Clever Application Gallery
features.description: A marketplace of vetted third-party education apps that connect directly through Clever.

features.title: Clever Portal
features.description: A unified dashboard where students and teachers launch all their connected learning tools.

features.title: Data privacy and compliance controls
features.description: Provides data-sharing agreements and controls designed around student data privacy regulations such as FERPA.

features.title: District-level admin controls
features.description: Gives IT administrators centralized management over which applications and data are shared with which schools or classes.

features.title: Instant Login for developers
features.description: Provides application developers with a standardized way to authenticate users without building their own SSO from scratch.

pros: Free for schools and districts to adopt, removing budget barriers to implementation
Extremely wide adoption across United States K-12 districts, meaning most major education apps already integrate with it
Badge and QR code login options make it accessible even for students who cannot yet read or type well
Centralizes rostering data so IT staff do not need to manually provision accounts across dozens of separate apps
Backed by Kahoot!, giving it more stable long-term funding and product investment than a standalone startup might have
cons: As a rostering and SSO layer, it depends on the accuracy of the underlying student information system data, so errors upstream can propagate
Districts and app developers alike must trust a single third-party vendor with sensitive student directory data
Some smaller or newer education apps may not yet be part of the Clever Application Gallery
Ongoing integration with Kahoot!'s broader product ecosystem means feature priorities are set at the group level rather than purely for SSO
Support and account provisioning issues can affect an entire district at once if sync jobs fail

faqs.question: What does Clever do?
faqs.answer: Clever is a K-12 single sign-on and data integration platform that lets students and teachers log in once to access hundreds of connected educational applications.

faqs.question: Who owns Clever?
faqs.answer: Clever Inc. is owned by Kahoot!, which completed its acquisition of Clever in September 2021.

faqs.question: Is Clever free for schools?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clever is free for K-12 schools and districts to adopt; Clever generates revenue mainly from the education-app developers that integrate with its platform.

faqs.question: How do students log in with Clever?
faqs.answer: Students can log in with a username and password, or with a physical badge or QR code that is especially useful for younger students who cannot yet type reliably.

faqs.question: What is the Clever Application Gallery?
faqs.answer: It is a marketplace of vetted third-party educational applications that districts can connect to their Clever instance for single sign-on access.

faqs.question: Does Clever handle student data privacy?
faqs.answer: Clever operates data-sharing agreements and privacy controls aligned with regulations such as FERPA, and districts control which applications receive which student data.

faqs.question: When was Clever founded?
faqs.answer: Clever was founded in 2012 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Is this the same as other unrelated products named Clever?
faqs.answer: No, this entry refers specifically to Clever Inc., the K-12 education SSO and rostering company now owned by Kahoot!, not any unrelated product or service that shares the generic name Clever.

use_cases.title: District-wide single sign-on rollout
use_cases.description: A school district connects its student information system to Clever so every student and teacher gets one login that works across all approved learning applications.

use_cases.title: Classroom app access for young students
use_cases.description: Elementary teachers use Clever badges or QR codes so students who cannot yet type can still log in to reading and math apps independently.

use_cases.title: Streamlined rostering for edtech vendors
use_cases.description: Education software companies integrate with Clever Instant Login and the Application Gallery to reach schools without building custom SSO and rostering infrastructure for every district.

enrichment.short_description: CleverTap is an all-in-one customer engagement and retention platform for mobile and web apps, combining real-time behavioral analytics, audience segmentation, and cross-channel campaign orchestration. Founded in 2013 (originally as WizRocket) and headquartered in San Francisco with a major hub in Mumbai, India, it serves over 2000 brands including Domino's, Levi's, and Puma. The platform bundles analytics, journey automation, A/B testing, and AI-driven personalization in a single dashboard.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered, usage-based subscription priced primarily on monthly active or tracked users, with custom enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Custom quote-based pricing; a free trial or demo is typically offered, but exact starting price is not publicly disclosed
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (with a major hub in Mumbai, India)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CleverTap is an all-in-one customer engagement and retention platform for mobile and web apps. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CleverTap is an all-in-one customer engagement and retention platform designed for mobile and web application businesses that need to understand user behavior and act on it through personalized, cross-channel messaging. Founded in 2013, originally under the name WizRocket, the company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with a major research, development, and operations hub in Mumbai, India, plus additional regional offices spanning Seattle, London, Sao Paulo, Bogota, Mexico, Amsterdam, Dubai, Bangalore, Singapore, Vietnam, and Jakarta, reflecting its strong footprint in high-growth and mobile-first markets. The platform combines several capabilities that are often sold separately by competitors: real-time behavioral analytics that track user actions inside apps and on websites as they happen, audience segmentation tools that build cohorts based on demographics, behavior, and lifecycle stage, RFM (recency, frequency, monetary value) analysis for identifying high-value and at-risk users, and cross-channel campaign orchestration that can send coordinated push notifications, in-app messages, email, SMS, WhatsApp messages, and web push notifications from a single interface. Its customer journey automation tools let marketing and growth teams build trigger-based flows that respond to user behavior automatically, such as sending a re-engagement message after a period of inactivity or a personalized onboarding sequence based on how a new user first interacts with an app. CleverTap also offers native SDKs for iOS, Android, and web that capture event data without requiring extensive custom engineering, along with A/B and multivariate testing tools for optimizing message content, timing, and channel selection, and AI-assisted personalization and recommendation features that aim to automate elements of send-time optimization and content selection. The company reports serving more than 2000 consumer brands globally across industries including retail, fintech, media and entertainment, and food delivery, with named customers including Domino's, Levi's, Puma, Swiggy, SonyLIV, Axis Bank, AirAsia, TD Bank, Ooredoo, Tesco, Croma, and Emirates NBD. CleverTap's pricing is not publicly published; the company uses a tiered, usage-based subscription model priced primarily around the number of monthly active or tracked users combined with the specific feature set a customer selects, and prospective customers typically need to request a custom quote or demo rather than self-serve sign up at a fixed listed price. In the competitive landscape, CleverTap is most commonly compared against Braze, MoEngage, and Iterable, with CleverTap's positioning generally emphasizing the breadth of its all-in-one analytics-plus-engagement approach and its particularly strong adoption among mobile-first consumer apps in emerging and high-growth markets across Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America, in contrast to competitors that may skew toward North American and European enterprise customers. Teams evaluating CleverTap in 2026 should expect a platform with substantial analytical depth and channel breadth, balanced against a real onboarding investment and usage-based costs that scale with active user volume.

long_form_blocks.heading: What CleverTap does
paragraphs: CleverTap is a customer engagement and retention platform built for mobile and web application businesses. It combines real-time behavioral analytics, audience segmentation, cross-channel campaign orchestration (push notifications, in-app messages, email, SMS, WhatsApp, and web push), and experimentation tools like A/B testing into a single platform, so growth and product marketing teams can analyze how users behave and then act on that data without stitching together separate analytics and messaging tools.

The platform is commonly used by consumer apps in retail, fintech, media and entertainment, and food delivery to reduce churn, personalize onboarding, and increase customer lifetime value through AI-assisted segmentation and recommendation features and other proprietary technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: History, funding, and scale
paragraphs: CleverTap was founded in 2013 (originally under the name WizRocket) and is headquartered in San Francisco, with a major research, development, and operations hub in Mumbai, India, plus additional offices across North America, Europe, the Middle East, Latin America, and Asia-Pacific. The company has raised substantial venture funding over multiple rounds and reports serving more than 2000 consumer brands, including Domino's, Levi's, Puma, Swiggy, and Emirates NBD.

long_form_blocks.heading: How CleverTap is positioned
paragraphs: CleverTap competes primarily with platforms such as Braze, MoEngage, and Iterable in the customer engagement and marketing automation space, differentiating itself around real-time analytics depth, its all-in-one approach that folds analytics and engagement into one product, and strong adoption among mobile-first consumer apps in emerging and high-growth markets.

features.title: RFM and cohort analysis

faqs.answer: CleverTap uses custom, usage-based pricing tied to monthly active or tracked users, and exact starting prices are not publicly disclosed; prospective customers request a quote.

enrichment.short_description: Clicky, at getclicky.com, is a real-time web analytics platform built by Roxr Software as a privacy-conscious alternative to Google Analytics. Founded in 2006 and based in Portland, Oregon, Clicky provides live visitor tracking, visitor-level session views, heatmaps, and uptime monitoring through a simple dashboard, with a free tier and affordable paid Pro plans.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium tiered subscription based on daily page views and number of websites tracked
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 3000 daily page views on 1 website; paid plans start at 9.99 USD per month
enrichment.company_size: Small team, fewer than 20 employees (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Clicky Web Analytics: Pricing and Features 2026
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clicky is a real-time web analytics platform and Google Analytics alternative. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clicky, found at getclicky.com, is a real-time web analytics platform built and operated by Roxr Software Ltd, a company founded in 2006 and based in Portland, Oregon. It was created and has continued to be positioned as a more immediate, human-readable alternative to Google Analytics, built around watching live visitor activity rather than only reviewing aggregated historical reports. The centerpiece of Clicky's design philosophy is its real-time dashboard, which shows page views, visitor counts, and traffic sources as they occur, plus a visitor-level feature called Spy, available on paid plans, that lets site owners follow an individual visitor's path through a website step by step. Clicky operates on a freemium pricing model. Its free tier covers one website with up to 3000 daily page views and includes core analytics such as content and referrer reports, basic segmentation, dashboard customization, widgets, and API access. Paid Pro plans start at 9.99 USD per month, or 79.99 USD billed annually (a 33 percent discount versus monthly billing), and expand coverage to 10 websites and 30000 daily page views while unlocking premium features including Spy, sub-user accounts, advanced filtering and segments, outbound link tracking, path analysis, download tracking, scheduled email reports, video analytics, engagement reports, custom data tracking, expanded historical data retention, data export, goals and revenue tracking, split testing, campaign tracking, and configurable alerts. A Pro Plus tier at 14.99 USD monthly (119.99 USD annually) adds heatmaps and uptime monitoring on top of all Pro features, while a Pro Platinum tier at 19.99 USD monthly (159.99 USD annually) raises limits to 30 websites and 100000 daily page views. A Custom enterprise tier, priced on request, scales up to 1000 websites and 20 million combined daily page views with all features included. New accounts of any tier receive a 21-day free trial of premium features covering up to 3 websites and 1,000,000 combined daily page views. Beyond raw traffic reporting, Clicky differentiates itself with built-in uptime monitoring that checks site availability from multiple geographic locations and alerts owners when a monitored site goes offline, plus privacy-oriented features including IP anonymization, consent management tooling, and a privacy mode that limits personal data collection, positioning it as a reasonable option for site owners who need to navigate GDPR and similar data protection regulations without adopting a heavier enterprise compliance suite. It integrates with common platforms including WordPress, Shopify, Squarespace, and BigCommerce, and exposes an API for developers who want to pull raw analytics data into other internal systems or dashboards. Clicky is best suited to small and mid-sized site owners, bloggers, and marketers who want an approachable, visitor-centric analytics tool with transparent, inexpensive pricing rather than the more abstracted, aggregate-first reporting style of larger enterprise analytics platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Clicky offers
paragraphs: Clicky, accessible at getclicky.com, is a real-time web analytics platform built and operated by Roxr Software Ltd, founded in 2006 and based in Portland, Oregon. It was designed as a more immediate, visitor-level alternative to Google Analytics, showing site owners live visitor activity, individual visitor session paths (called Spy in paid plans), heatmaps, and uptime monitoring in one dashboard.

Rather than emphasizing only aggregated reports, Clicky's interface is built around watching what individual visitors are doing on a site in real time, which appeals to smaller site owners, bloggers, and marketers who want an at-a-glance, human-readable view of traffic rather than a heavily abstracted reporting suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and plan tiers
paragraphs: Clicky uses a freemium model. The free plan covers one website with up to 3000 daily page views and core analytics. Paid Pro plans start at 9.99 USD per month (or 79.99 USD annually) and unlock premium features such as visitor-level Spy view, advanced segmentation, campaign tracking, goals and revenue tracking, and data export, while higher Pro Plus and Pro Platinum tiers add heatmaps, uptime monitoring, and support for more websites and higher page-view volumes. Custom enterprise plans scale up to 1000 websites and 20 million daily page views.

long_form_blocks.heading: Privacy features and integrations
paragraphs: Clicky offers privacy-oriented features including IP anonymization, consent management, and a privacy mode that limits personal data collection, which has made it a common choice for site owners navigating GDPR and similar regulations. It integrates with platforms such as WordPress, Shopify, Squarespace, and BigCommerce, and exposes an API for developers who want to pull analytics data into other systems.

Free tier is genuinely usable for smaller sites with up to 3000 daily page views
Privacy-friendly options such as IP anonymization and consent management support GDPR compliance
Uptime monitoring and heatmaps are bundled in rather than requiring separate third-party tools
Affordable entry-level paid pricing compared to many enterprise analytics platforms

Advanced features like Spy, heatmaps, and uptime monitoring are locked behind paid tiers
Daily page view limits on lower tiers can be restrictive for growing sites

Custom and enterprise pricing above the Pro Platinum tier is not transparently published and requires direct inquiry

faqs.question: What is Clicky used for?
faqs.answer: Clicky is a real-time web analytics platform used to track live visitor activity, individual visitor sessions, heatmaps, and site uptime as an alternative to Google Analytics.

faqs.question: Is Clicky free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clicky offers a free plan covering one website with up to 3000 daily page views; paid Pro plans start at 9.99 USD per month.

faqs.question: Who makes Clicky?
faqs.answer: Clicky is made by Roxr Software Ltd, founded in 2006 and based in Portland, Oregon.

faqs.question: Does Clicky support GDPR compliance?
faqs.answer: Clicky offers IP anonymization, consent management, and a privacy mode designed to help site owners meet GDPR and similar privacy requirements.

faqs.question: What is the Spy feature in Clicky?
faqs.answer: Spy is a Clicky feature available on paid plans that lets site owners watch individual visitor sessions and page paths in real time.

faqs.question: Does Clicky offer uptime monitoring?
faqs.answer: Yes, uptime monitoring is included starting with the Pro Plus plan, checking site availability from multiple locations and alerting owners of downtime.

faqs.question: How does Clicky compare to Google Analytics?
faqs.answer: Clicky focuses more on real-time, visitor-level tracking and a simpler dashboard, while Google Analytics offers deeper aggregate reporting and is free at any traffic volume.

faqs.question: Can Clicky track e-commerce revenue?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clicky's Pro plans include goals and revenue tracking to attribute conversions and revenue to specific traffic sources and campaigns.
