use_cases.description: An agency managing multiple client sites uses Clicky's custom plan to monitor uptime and track campaign-level traffic and revenue across dozens of properties from one dashboard.

features: Up to 20 million daily page views

enrichment.short_description: The Climate Corporation is a digital agriculture company founded in 2006 in San Francisco (originally as WeatherBill) that now operates as a wholly owned subsidiary of Bayer within its Crop Science division. Its flagship product, Climate FieldView, is a precision-farming software and hardware platform that helps farmers collect, visualize, and act on field data such as yield, planting, spraying, and soil health information across millions of acres worldwide.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription (such as FieldView Plus) plus optional FieldView Drive hardware purchase; exact public pricing is not published and varies by region and dealer
enrichment.starting_price: Not publicly disclosed; FieldView offers a free basic tier alongside paid subscription tiers sold through Bayer's dealer network
enrichment.company_size: Subsidiary of Bayer AG (Bayer employs approximately 90000 to 100000 people globally); Climate Corporation-specific headcount is not publicly disclosed
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (operating within Bayer Crop Science, whose parent Bayer AG is headquartered in Leverkusen, Germany)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: The Climate Corporation, maker of Climate FieldView, is Bayer's digital agriculture platform. See features, pricing notes, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: The Climate Corporation is a digital agriculture company founded in 2006 in San Francisco, California, by former Google employees David Friedberg and Siraj Khaliq under the original name WeatherBill. The company initially sold weather-based insurance products to businesses such as ski resorts and large event venues before pivoting around 2010 to focus exclusively on agriculture, using weather and soil data to help farmers manage risk. In October 2013, agricultural giant Monsanto acquired The Climate Corporation for approximately 1.1 billion USD, and when Bayer AG completed its much larger acquisition of Monsanto in 2018, The Climate Corporation became a subsidiary within Bayer's Crop Science division, where it remains headquartered in San Francisco and continues to operate as Bayer's primary digital agriculture and data-science unit. The company's flagship customer-facing product is Climate FieldView, a precision farming and digital agriculture platform that has grown into one of the most widely used farm data platforms globally, competing directly with John Deere Operations Center, Trimble Ag, and Granular by Corteva Agriscience for the central role of managing a farm's field-level data. FieldView collects data throughout the growing season primarily through FieldView Drive, a hardware device that plugs into compatible farm equipment across multiple major machinery brands and automatically captures planting, spraying, and harvest data as operations happen, supplemented by manually imported records and satellite or aerial field imagery. Farmers use the resulting data to analyze and compare yield performance across different hybrids, fields, and management zones, evaluate the return on investment of specific seed varieties and crop protection products, and build variable-rate prescriptions that tailor seeding density, fertilizer, and other inputs to specific zones within a field rather than applying a single rate uniformly. The platform also supports in-season monitoring of crop health through imagery-based field health tools, centralizes multi-year field records in one place, and enables farmers to share access with trusted independent crop advisors and agronomists for collaborative analysis. Additionally, FieldView connects to Bayer's regenerative agriculture and carbon initiatives, such as ForGround by Bayer, letting farmers participate in sustainability-linked programs using the same field data already captured for agronomic purposes. As of 2026, Bayer continues actively investing in FieldView, including hardware upgrades to FieldView Drive for improved processing power and connection stability, expanded features for evaluating applied product performance and yield analysis, and continued international expansion into additional markets including South Africa and parts of East Africa such as Kenya, alongside its established base in North America, South America, and Europe. Public list pricing for FieldView subscription tiers, such as FieldView Plus, is not transparently published online; the platform is sold through Bayer's dealer network and varies by region, though a free basic tier of FieldView has historically been available alongside paid subscription tiers that unlock additional data capacity and advanced analytical features.

long_form_blocks.heading: From WeatherBill to Climate FieldView
paragraphs: The Climate Corporation was founded in 2006 in San Francisco by former Google employees David Friedberg and Siraj Khaliq under the name WeatherBill, initially selling weather-based insurance products before pivoting to focus exclusively on agriculture around 2010. Monsanto acquired the company in October 2013 for approximately 1.1 billion USD, and when Bayer completed its acquisition of Monsanto in 2018, The Climate Corporation became part of Bayer's Crop Science division.

Its flagship product today is Climate FieldView, a digital farming platform that has become one of the most widely used farm data platforms in the world, competing with John Deere Operations Center, Trimble Ag, and Granular by Corteva Agriscience for farmers' field data relationships.

long_form_blocks.heading: What FieldView does for farmers
paragraphs: FieldView collects and visualizes field-level data throughout the growing season, including planting, spraying, and harvest information gathered automatically via the FieldView Drive hardware device plugged into farm equipment, plus manually imported data and satellite or aerial imagery. Farmers use FieldView to compare yield performance across hybrids and fields, evaluate the return on applied inputs like seed and fertilizer, build variable-rate prescriptions, and monitor soil health and in-season crop conditions.

The platform also supports participation in Bayer's regenerative agriculture and carbon programs, such as ForGround by Bayer, and continues to expand its integrations with independent crop advisors, agronomists, and third-party farm management tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Current status as of 2026
paragraphs: As of 2026, Climate FieldView continues to operate as Bayer's core digital agriculture platform, with active investment in FieldView Drive hardware upgrades, expanded international availability including markets such as South Africa and Kenya, and growing emphasis on variable-rate prescriptions and digital soil health tools. The Climate Corporation brand persists mainly as the legal corporate entity, while Climate FieldView is the customer-facing product brand farmers actually interact with.

features.title: FieldView Drive hardware
features.description: A device that plugs into farm equipment to automatically capture planting, spraying, and harvest data.

features.title: Yield analysis
features.description: Visualizes and compares yield performance across fields, hybrids, and management zones.

features.title: Variable-rate prescriptions
features.description: Builds seeding, fertilizer, and crop protection prescriptions tailored to specific zones within a field.

features.title: Field health imagery
features.description: Uses satellite and aerial imagery to monitor in-season crop health and detect problem areas.

features.title: Data storage and field records
features.description: Centralizes historical field data across multiple growing seasons in one platform.

features.title: Product performance evaluation
features.description: Helps farmers assess the return on investment of specific seed hybrids and crop inputs.

features.title: Advisor and agronomist collaboration
features.description: Lets farmers share field data with trusted crop advisors and agronomists for joint analysis.

features.title: Carbon and regenerative agriculture program integration
features.description: Connects FieldView data to programs like ForGround by Bayer for sustainability-linked incentives.

pros: Backed by Bayer's scale and R&D investment, giving it long-term stability and continued feature development
Automatic data capture through FieldView Drive reduces manual data entry for farmers
Wide equipment compatibility across multiple farm machinery brands, not limited to a single manufacturer's equipment
Strong yield and input performance analysis tools that help farmers make agronomic decisions
Integration with regenerative agriculture and carbon programs offers farmers additional revenue opportunities
cons: Public pricing is not transparently published, requiring farmers to work through a dealer or Bayer representative to get a quote
As a subsidiary of a large multinational, some farmers have data-ownership and privacy concerns about sharing detailed field data
Full functionality often depends on purchasing or renting the FieldView Drive hardware, adding to the cost of adoption
Competing platforms tied to specific equipment brands, like John Deere Operations Center, may integrate more tightly with that brand's own machinery
Some advanced features are region-dependent and not uniformly available in every country FieldView operates in

faqs.question: What is Climate FieldView?
faqs.answer: Climate FieldView is a digital agriculture and precision farming platform, owned by Bayer through The Climate Corporation, that helps farmers collect, visualize, and act on field-level data.

faqs.question: Who owns The Climate Corporation?
faqs.answer: The Climate Corporation is a wholly owned subsidiary of Bayer AG, having been acquired by Monsanto in 2013 and then absorbed into Bayer when Bayer acquired Monsanto in 2018.

faqs.question: When was The Climate Corporation founded?
faqs.answer: It was founded in 2006 in San Francisco, originally under the name WeatherBill, before pivoting to agriculture-focused technology.

faqs.question: How much does Climate FieldView cost?
faqs.answer: Public list pricing is not disclosed; FieldView is sold through Bayer and its dealer network with a free basic tier and paid subscription tiers such as FieldView Plus that vary by region.

faqs.question: What is FieldView Drive?
faqs.answer: FieldView Drive is the hardware device farmers plug into compatible equipment to automatically capture planting, spraying, and harvest data for use in the FieldView platform.

faqs.question: Does FieldView work with equipment from brands other than Bayer?
faqs.answer: Yes, FieldView Drive and the platform are designed to be compatible with equipment across multiple major farm machinery brands.

faqs.question: What is the difference between The Climate Corporation and Climate FieldView?
faqs.answer: The Climate Corporation is the legal corporate entity and subsidiary of Bayer, while Climate FieldView is the customer-facing digital farming product brand that farmers actually use.

faqs.question: Does FieldView support sustainability or carbon programs?
faqs.answer: Yes, FieldView data can connect to Bayer's regenerative agriculture initiatives such as ForGround by Bayer for sustainability-linked programs.

use_cases.title: Comparing hybrid performance across fields
use_cases.description: A row-crop farmer uses FieldView's yield analysis to compare how different seed hybrids performed across multiple fields and soil types in a growing season.

use_cases.title: Building variable-rate prescriptions
use_cases.description: A farm operation uses FieldView to build zone-specific seeding and fertilizer prescriptions based on historical yield and soil data.

use_cases.title: Sharing field data with a crop advisor
use_cases.description: A farmer grants a trusted agronomist access to their FieldView account so the advisor can review in-season imagery and recommend adjustments remotely.
use_cases.audience: Independent crop advisors and their farmer clients

enrichment.short_description: ClipGrab is a free video downloader and converter application created by German developer Philipp Schmieder, first released in November 2007. It lets users download videos from sites such as YouTube, Vimeo, and Dailymotion and convert them to formats including MP4, MP3, and OGG. As of 2026 the software continues to be maintained, though users have reported intermittent YouTube-download breakage and third-party software bundled with some Windows installers, so downloading only from the official clipgrab.org site is recommended.
enrichment.company_size: Independent developer project, not a company; exact team size not publicly disclosed
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable; independent software project with its developer based in Germany
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClipGrab is a free video downloader and converter for YouTube and more. See 2026 features, safety notes, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClipGrab is a free video downloader and format-converter application originally created by German software developer Philipp Schmieder and first released on 5 November 2007 as a personal project. It is distributed as a standalone desktop application for Windows, macOS, and Linux rather than as a browser extension, and one of its defining usability features is clipboard monitoring: the application watches the system clipboard and automatically recognizes when a user has copied a URL from a supported video site, offering to begin the download without further setup. ClipGrab supports downloading from a range of video platforms, historically including YouTube, Vimeo, Dailymotion, and several smaller or now-defunct sites such as College Humor, Clipfish, MyVideo, and Sevenload, and it includes a built-in search feature that lets users find videos directly inside the application rather than needing to first locate and copy a URL from a browser. After downloading, ClipGrab can convert the resulting file into several common formats, including MPEG-4 (MP4), MP3, OGG Vorbis, OGG Theora, and WMV, and it can extract audio-only files from video downloads, making it useful for users who want to save just the audio track of a video such as a lecture, interview, or music performance. The application includes a format-detection feature designed to avoid unnecessary re-encoding when the source and target formats already match, preserving the original download quality rather than degrading it through an avoidable conversion step. As of 2026, ClipGrab's practical reliability, particularly for YouTube downloads, has been inconsistent: the site's frequent internal changes to how video streams are served have repeatedly broken ClipGrab's YouTube parsing logic, and while the developer released an update on 24 April 2026 intended to address this, user reports afterward indicate parsing problems persisted for at least some users into the following months. Separately, in June 2026, testers and users reported that the standard Windows installer, built on the InstallCore installer framework, silently bundled additional unwanted software alongside ClipGrab itself, specifically an antivirus product called RAV Endpoint Protection, along with other adware toolbars and browser-hijacking components reported by users on platforms including Reddit and Trustpilot. Anyone installing ClipGrab in 2026 should therefore download only directly from the official clipgrab.org website, read every screen of the installer carefully, and decline any bundled software offers rather than clicking through default options. As with any general-purpose video downloading tool, using ClipGrab to save content a user does not have rights to may violate the terms of service of the source platform or applicable copyright law, and it is intended primarily for saving content users are authorized to download, such as their own uploads or explicitly downloadable, licensed material.

long_form_blocks.heading: What ClipGrab is
paragraphs: ClipGrab is a free video downloader and format-converter application originally created by German developer Philipp Schmieder and first released in November 2007. It runs as a standalone desktop application on Windows, macOS, and Linux rather than a browser extension, and it can monitor the clipboard so it automatically detects when a user copies a supported video URL.

The tool downloads videos from platforms including YouTube, Vimeo, Dailymotion, and other sites that expose downloadable video streams, then optionally converts the downloaded file into common formats such as MP4, MP3, OGG Vorbis, OGG Theora, or WMV, making it a popular free option for users who want to save online videos for offline viewing or extract audio tracks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Reliability and safety considerations in 2026
paragraphs: Because ClipGrab depends on parsing constantly changing streaming-site page structures, particularly YouTube's, its core download functionality has experienced intermittent breakage throughout 2026; the developer pushed an update in April 2026, though users have continued to report parsing issues afterward. Prospective users should expect some instability with YouTube downloads specifically and check for the latest version before relying on it.

There have also been reports in 2026 of the official Windows installer bundling third-party software, including an antivirus product and other browser add-ons, alongside the core application. Users who choose to install ClipGrab should download only from the official clipgrab.org site, review each installer screen carefully, and decline any bundled offers they do not want.

long_form_blocks.heading: Legal and usage notes
paragraphs: As with any video-downloading tool, using ClipGrab to save copyrighted content may violate the terms of service of the source platform and, depending on jurisdiction and use, applicable copyright law. Users are generally expected to only download content they have the rights to save, such as their own uploads or content explicitly licensed for download.

features.title: Clipboard URL monitoring
features.description: Automatically detects supported video URLs copied to the clipboard and offers to download them.

features.title: Multi-site video downloading
features.description: Supports downloading from YouTube, Vimeo, Dailymotion, and other compatible video platforms.

features.title: Format conversion
features.description: Converts downloaded videos to MP4, MP3, OGG Vorbis, OGG Theora, or WMV.

features.title: Audio extraction
features.description: Converts video downloads directly into audio-only files.

features.title: Built-in video search
features.description: Lets users search for videos from within the application interface.

features.title: Smart format detection
features.description: Avoids unnecessary re-encoding when the source format already matches the desired output, preserving original quality.

features.title: Cross-platform desktop app
features.description: Available for Windows, macOS, and Linux as a standalone application.

features.title: No account required
features.description: Works without requiring users to sign up or log in.

pros: Completely free to use with no subscription or account requirement
Simple, focused interface that is easy for non-technical users to operate
Combines downloading and format conversion in a single tool rather than requiring separate software
Available across Windows, macOS, and Linux
Clipboard monitoring makes starting a download fast and low-friction
cons: YouTube download functionality has been unstable in 2026 due to frequent site-side changes
Some Windows installers have bundled unwanted third-party software, including antivirus products and browser add-ons
Development pace and update frequency can lag behind changes made by major video platforms
Limited site support compared to more actively maintained command-line tools
Using it to download copyrighted content may violate platform terms of service or copyright law depending on use and jurisdiction

faqs.question: Is ClipGrab free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClipGrab is free software with no subscription or account required.

faqs.question: Does ClipGrab work with YouTube in 2026?
faqs.answer: ClipGrab supports YouTube downloads, but functionality has been intermittently unstable throughout 2026 due to changes on YouTube's side; users may need to update to the latest version.

faqs.question: Is ClipGrab safe to install?
faqs.answer: The core application is free and long-standing, but some 2026 Windows installers have bundled unwanted third-party software, so users should download only from the official clipgrab.org site and carefully review installer prompts.

faqs.question: What video formats can ClipGrab convert to?
faqs.answer: ClipGrab can convert downloaded videos to MP4, MP3, OGG Vorbis, OGG Theora, and WMV.

faqs.question: What platforms does ClipGrab support downloading from?
faqs.answer: ClipGrab supports YouTube, Vimeo, Dailymotion, and several other video platforms with downloadable streams.

faqs.question: Who created ClipGrab?
faqs.answer: ClipGrab was created by German developer Philipp Schmieder, first released in November 2007.

faqs.question: Does ClipGrab work on Mac and Linux?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClipGrab is available for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: Is it legal to use ClipGrab?
faqs.answer: Using ClipGrab to download content you do not have rights to may violate a platform's terms of service or copyright law; it is intended for downloading content you are authorized to save.

use_cases.title: Saving videos for offline viewing
use_cases.description: A user downloads video content they have rights to for offline viewing while traveling without a reliable internet connection.
use_cases.audience: Casual users needing offline video access

use_cases.title: Extracting audio from video content
use_cases.description: A user converts a video download into an MP3 audio file to listen to a lecture or music track without the video component.

use_cases.title: Archiving personal video content
use_cases.description: A content creator downloads and converts their own uploaded videos for backup and archival purposes outside the original hosting platform.

enrichment.short_description: Clockwork Micro is a Seattle-based geospatial technology company, founded in 2021 by Brendan Farrell, that provides web-mapping and cartography APIs rather than semiconductor hardware. Its product line includes an Elevation API, Basemap and Terrain Tile services, USGS Topo Maps, Map Maker (a low-code map-building tool), and DB2Vector (SQL-to-vector-tile conversion), aimed at developers who need to embed custom maps without building cartography infrastructure from scratch.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered, usage-based API pricing with a free tier and metered overage
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available (50000 API and tile requests per service); paid plans start at 25 USD per month
enrichment.company_size: Small startup, fewer than 20 employees (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: Clockwork Micro: Mapping API Pricing and Features 2026
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Clockwork Micro offers elevation, basemap, and terrain mapping APIs for developers. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Clockwork Micro is a geospatial technology company, not a semiconductor or wireless-chip manufacturer despite what its name might suggest to some searchers. Founded in 2021 by Brendan Farrell and based in Seattle, Washington, the company builds web-mapping and cartography application programming interfaces (APIs) aimed at software developers and organizations who need to embed custom, styled maps into products without building and operating their own map-tile infrastructure from scratch. Its stated mission is to abstract away the time-consuming technical work involved in serving a map built on custom data, letting developers focus on how they want their map to look and what it should present rather than on the underlying tile-serving, projection, and rendering pipeline. Clockwork Micro's product line spans several distinct but related tools. Its Elevation API returns terrain elevation data for a given location or region, useful for applications like hiking route planning or terrain visualization. Its Basemap and Terrain Tile services provide foundational and terrain-focused map tile layers that developers can style and build on top of. It also serves USGS Topo Maps, including official United States Geological Survey 7.5-minute topographic quadrangle tiles, which are widely used in outdoor recreation, land-use, and government mapping applications. For less technical users, Map Maker offers a low-code interface for building interactive, modular maps directly from custom data, while a companion Simple Styler tool provides a visual interface for adjusting map layer colors, opacity, and boundary appearance without writing styling code by hand. A more developer-oriented tool, DB2Vector, converts the results of SQL queries directly into vector tile endpoints, letting teams that already store geospatial data in a relational database expose it as map tiles without standing up a separate tile-generation pipeline. Clockwork Micro's pricing follows a tiered, usage-based API model with four levels: a free Personal tier including 50000 API and tile requests per service; a Standard tier at 25 USD per month raising that to 100000 requests per service; a Commercial tier at 195 USD per month including 1000000 requests per service at a reduced overage rate of 6 cents per 1000 additional requests, compared to 8 cents per 1000 on the lower tiers; and a custom Enterprise tier for organizations with larger or contractually negotiated needs. Because Clockwork Micro is a small and relatively young company compared to established mapping platforms such as Mapbox, Esri, or Google Maps Platform, prospective customers evaluating it for production or mission-critical use should independently verify current API uptime, long-term roadmap commitments, and company stability directly with Clockwork Micro rather than assuming the maturity of a larger incumbent provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Clockwork Micro actually does
paragraphs: Despite a name that might suggest semiconductor or wireless chip hardware, Clockwork Micro is a geospatial technology company that builds web-mapping and cartography APIs. Founded in 2021 by Brendan Farrell and based in Seattle, Washington, the company focuses on abstracting away the time-consuming infrastructure work involved in serving custom maps, so developers can focus on how their data should look rather than how tile servers and elevation datasets work under the hood.

Its product line includes an Elevation API for terrain height data, Basemap and Terrain Tile services, USGS Topo Maps (including 7.5-minute topographic quadrangles), a low-code tool called Map Maker for building interactive maps, a Simple Styler interface for adjusting map layer appearance, and DB2Vector, which converts SQL query results directly into vector tile endpoints.

long_form_blocks.heading: Target audience and pricing structure
paragraphs: Clockwork Micro targets software developers, GIS professionals, and organizations that need to embed custom, styled maps into applications without operating their own map-tile infrastructure. Its pricing follows a tiered, usage-based API model: a free Personal tier includes 50000 requests per service, a Standard tier at 25 USD per month raises that to 100000 requests, a Commercial tier at 195 USD per month includes 1000000 requests at a lower per-unit overage rate, and a custom Enterprise tier is available for larger-scale or contractually negotiated needs.

Because the company is a small, relatively young startup rather than an established mapping incumbent like Mapbox or Esri, prospective users should verify current API stability, uptime track record, and long-term roadmap directly with Clockwork Micro before committing to it for mission-critical production mapping needs.

features.title: Elevation API
features.description: Provides programmatic access to terrain elevation data for a given location or area.

features.title: Basemap tiles
features.description: Supplies foundational map tile layers for building custom map applications.

features.title: Terrain Tiles
features.description: Offers terrain-focused map tile data suitable for hillshading and topographic visualization.

features.title: USGS Topo Maps
features.description: Serves official USGS 7.5-minute topographic quadrangle map tiles.

features.title: Map Maker
features.description: A low-code interface for building interactive, modular maps without writing mapping infrastructure from scratch.

features.title: Simple Styler
features.description: A UI tool for adjusting map layer colors, opacity, and boundary styling.

features.title: DB2Vector
features.description: Converts SQL query results directly into vector tile endpoints for custom data visualization.

features.title: Tiered usage-based API pricing
features.description: Offers a free tier plus metered paid tiers that scale with request volume.

pros: Free Personal tier makes it accessible for developers to test and prototype before committing to a paid plan
Focused product set covering elevation, basemaps, terrain, and topo maps that is easy for developers to evaluate quickly
Low-code Map Maker tool lowers the barrier for non-GIS-specialist developers to build maps
Transparent, published tiered pricing compared to some competitors that require a sales conversation
DB2Vector offers a relatively unusual and useful capability for teams already storing geospatial data in SQL databases
cons: Much smaller company and community than established mapping platforms like Mapbox, Esri, or Google Maps Platform
Founded relatively recently in 2021, so its long-term track record and API stability are less proven
Limited public information about company size, funding, and enterprise customer base
Smaller feature surface than full GIS platforms that offer advanced spatial analysis tools
Overage and enterprise pricing details require direct engagement with the company for full clarity at scale

faqs.question: What does Clockwork Micro make?
faqs.answer: Clockwork Micro makes web-mapping and cartography APIs, including an Elevation API, Basemap and Terrain Tiles, USGS Topo Maps, and low-code map-building tools; it is not a semiconductor or wireless chip company despite its name.

faqs.question: When was Clockwork Micro founded?
faqs.answer: Clockwork Micro was founded in 2021 by Brendan Farrell.

faqs.question: Where is Clockwork Micro based?
faqs.answer: Clockwork Micro is based in Seattle, Washington, United States.

faqs.question: Does Clockwork Micro have a free tier?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Personal tier is free and includes 50000 API and tile requests per service.

faqs.question: How much do paid Clockwork Micro plans cost?
faqs.answer: The Standard plan starts at 25 USD per month for 100000 requests per service, and the Commercial plan is 195 USD per month for 1000000 requests per service with lower overage rates.

faqs.question: What is Map Maker?
faqs.answer: Map Maker is Clockwork Micro's low-code interface for building interactive, modular maps from custom data without needing to build map-tile infrastructure from scratch.

faqs.question: What is DB2Vector?
faqs.answer: DB2Vector is a Clockwork Micro tool that converts SQL query results directly into vector tile endpoints for mapping applications.

faqs.question: Who is Clockwork Micro's target customer?
faqs.answer: Clockwork Micro primarily targets software developers, GIS professionals, and organizations that want to embed custom maps into applications without operating their own map infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Adding elevation data to an outdoor recreation app
use_cases.description: A hiking or trail-mapping app integrates Clockwork Micro's Elevation API to show elevation profiles along a route.
use_cases.audience: Outdoor recreation and fitness app developers

use_cases.title: Building a custom-styled map without a GIS team
use_cases.description: A startup without dedicated GIS engineers uses Map Maker and Simple Styler to launch a branded interactive map quickly.

use_cases.title: Visualizing SQL-stored geospatial data
use_cases.description: A logistics company uses DB2Vector to turn its existing SQL database of delivery zones directly into vector map tiles without building a separate tile-serving pipeline.
